title: Psychiatric Intake — Initial Evaluation
label: Intake Evaluation

goals: Build therapeutic alliance and assess safety

text:
Therapist: Thanks for coming in today. I know reaching out for help is often the hardest step. What brings you in?

Patient: I've been... not okay for a while. My wife finally basically gave me an ultimatum. She said either you get help or I don't know what happens to us. So I'm here.

Therapist: I appreciate your honesty about what brought you in. Even if the push came from outside, you made the choice to show up. How long have you been not okay?

Patient: If I'm being real? Probably two years. But it got bad — like really bad — about six months ago. I got passed over for a promotion I'd been working toward for three years. And something just... broke.

Therapist: Can you describe what 'really bad' looks like for you day to day?

Patient: I don't sleep. Maybe four hours a night if I'm lucky. I'm at work but I'm not really there — I sit at my desk and stare at the screen and nothing happens. I used to love my job. I'm a software engineer and I used to love the problem-solving and now I look at code and feel nothing. I come home and I don't talk to my wife or my kids. They're eating dinner and I'm in the bedroom with the lights off.

Therapist: That sounds profoundly isolating. What do you do in the bedroom with the lights off?

Patient: Nothing. I lay there. Sometimes I'm on my phone but not really looking at anything. My mind races but I'm also completely empty. It's a weird feeling to describe.

Therapist: That combination — racing thoughts and emotional numbness at the same time — is actually something we hear a lot. It can be part of a depressive episode, especially when sleep deprivation is significant. Have you had any periods in your life, even briefly, when you felt unusually energized, needed very little sleep, or made decisions you later regretted?

Patient: No, nothing like that. The opposite — I've always been kind of cautious. Slow to act.

Therapist: Helpful to know. I want to ask you directly about safety. Have you had any thoughts of harming yourself or thoughts that you'd be better off dead?

Patient: Not... actively. Like I'm not planning anything. But I've had thoughts like 'everyone would be better without me.' That it would be easier to just not exist. I don't want to do anything about it — I've got two kids. But the thought comes.

Therapist: I'm really glad you told me that. Those thoughts are called passive suicidal ideation and they're something we take seriously without panicking — they tell me you're in real pain. Before you leave today we're going to make a safety plan together. You should never leave a first session without one. Can you tell me about your support network — who outside your wife knows what's going on?

Patient: Nobody. I haven't told my parents, my brother doesn't know. I'm the person everyone thinks has it together.

Therapist: That isolation of the illness from your support network is actually one of the things that makes it heavier. We'll work on that. Based on what you've shared today — the sleep disruption, the anhedonia, the cognitive shutdown, the social withdrawal, and the passive ideation — I want to share my initial clinical impression with you. Does that feel okay?

Patient: Yeah. Please.

Therapist: What you're describing sounds consistent with a major depressive episode, possibly severe given the duration and functional impairment. The two-year history is important context — this isn't a situational blip from the promotion. Losing that promotion was likely the straw that broke something that had been bending for a while. The good news: this is treatable. Most people with what you're describing respond well to a combination of therapy and, often, medication. I want to discuss both. Do you have a primary care doctor?

Patient: Yeah, Dr. Reyes. I haven't seen her in two years though.

Therapist: I'd recommend we reach back out to her to discuss a medication evaluation. In the meantime, let's start therapy weekly and build from there. I need you to do one thing before next week: tell one person — just one — that you're getting help. It can be your brother, your parents, anyone. Removing the secret takes weight off. Can we agree on that?

Patient: Yeah. I can do that.

Therapist: Good. Now let's do the safety plan before you go.
